**Principle:** Separates ions based on charge using resin with opposite charge. - **Food Applications:** Analysis of amino acids, organic acids (citric, lactic, acetic), nitrate/nitrite in cured meats. ### 2.2.7 Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC) / Size Exclusion - **Principle:** Separates molecules by size using porous gel beads. - **Food Applications:** Molecular weight distribution of starch, proteins, pectins, hydrocolloids. ### 2.2.8 Capillary Electrophoresis (CE) - **Principle:** Separates ions in a capillary under high electric field based on charge/size ratio. - **Food Applications:** Analysis of organic acids, vitamins, chiral separation of food additives. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Description | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | Beer-Lambert Law: Absorbance equals molar absorptivity × concentration × path length | UV-Vis quantification | A (absorbance), ε (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), c (mol/L), l (cm) | | $T = I \div I_0$ | Transmittance: Ratio of transmitted to incident light | Spectroscopy | Dimensionless | | $A = -\log_{10} T$ | Relation between absorbance and transmittance | Spectroscopy | Dimensionless | | $R_f = d_s \div d_f$ | Retention factor: distance moved by solute / distance moved by solvent front | TLC/Paper Chromatography | Dimensionless | | $N = 16 \cdot (t_R \div W_b)^2$ | Number of theoretical plates — column efficiency | Chromatography | Dimensionless | | $H = L \div N$ | Height equivalent to a theoretical plate (HETP) | Chromatography | cm or mm | | $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1}) \div (W_1 + W_2)$ | Resolution between two adjacent peaks | Chromatography | Dimensionless | | $k' = (t_R - t_M) \div t_M$ | Capacity factor (retention factor in column) | Chromatography | Dimensionless | | $\alpha = k_2' \div k_1'$ | Selectivity factor (alpha) | Chromatography | Dimensionless | | $\nu = c \div \lambda$ | Wavenumber = 1/wavelength | IR Spectroscopy | cm⁻¹ | | $E = h \cdot \nu$ | Energy of photon (Planck's relation) | All spectroscopy | J or eV | | $c = \lambda \cdot \nu$ | Speed of light = wavelength × frequency | All spectroscopy | m/s | | $m \div z$ | Mass-to-charge ratio in mass spectrometry | MS | Thomson or Da/e | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In the food industry, these techniques are deployed in **Quality Control (QC) laboratories, R&D centers of food companies, regulatory agencies (FSSAI, FDA, EFSA), and third-party testing labs** to ensure food safety, nutritional labeling, adulteration detection, and compliance with international food standards (Codex Alimentarius, BIS, ISO). ## 2.4 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method | Analyte Type | Recommended Spectroscopy | Recommended Chromatography | |---|---|---| | Metals (Pb, Cd, As) | AAS, ICP-OES | IC (Ion Chromatography) | | Vitamins | UV-Vis, Fluorescence | HPLC (RP), LC-MS | | Pesticide Residues | MS (as detector) | GC-ECD, LC-MS/MS | | Fatty Acids | FTIR | GC-FID (as FAMEs) | | Food Colors | UV-Vis | TLC, HPLC | | Mycotoxins | Fluorescence | HPLC-FLD, TLC | | Sugars | RI detection | HPLC-RID, IC | | Proteins | UV-Vis (280 nm) | SDS-PAGE, GPC | | Volatile Flavors | MS | GC-MS, HS-SPME-GC | | Adulterants | NMR, FTIR | HPLC, GC | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law (KTU High-Priority) The Beer-Lambert Law is the cornerstone of quantitative UV-Vis spectroscopy used in food analysis (e.g., for total phenolics by Folin-Ciocalteu method, color intensity, preservative estimation). ### Step 1: Consider a beam of monochromatic light Let the incident intensity be $I_0$ and after passing through a solution of thickness $dl$, the intensity reduces to $I$. ### Step 2: The fractional decrease in intensity The fractional decrease in intensity is proportional to the thickness of the absorbing layer and the concentration $c$ of the absorbing species: $$-\frac{dI}{I} = k \cdot c \cdot dl$$ Here, $k$ is the proportionality constant. ### Step 3: Integration over the entire path length Integrate both sides from $I_0$ to $I$ on the left and from $0$ to $l$ on the right: $$\int_{I_0}^{I} -\frac{dI}{I} = \int_{0}^{l} k \cdot c \, dl$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate the integrals Since $c$ is constant throughout the path: $$-\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = k \cdot c \cdot l$$ ### Step 5: Convert to base-10 logarithm Using $-\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$ and substituting the constant $2.303 \cdot k = \varepsilon$ (molar absorptivity): $$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ ### Step 6: Define Absorbance Absorbance is defined as $A = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$, which gives us the final form: $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the most-asked derivation in KTU Food Analysis papers for UV-Vis quantification of preservatives, pigments, and total phenolics. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Standard **Problem:** A food sample solution containing riboflavin (Vitamin B₂) shows an absorbance of 0.485 at 444 nm in a 1.0 cm cuvette. The molar absorptivity of riboflavin at 444 nm is **12,200 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**. Calculate the concentration of riboflavin in mol/L. ### Step 1: Identify the given values - $A = 0.485$ - $\varepsilon = 12{,}200 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$ - $l = 1.0 \text{ cm}$ ### Step 2: Apply Beer-Lambert Law $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ ### Step 3: Rearrange for concentration $$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute the values $$c = \frac{0.485}{12{,}200 \times 1.0}$$ ### Step 5: Compute the result $$c = 3.975 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$ ### Step 6: Convert to mg/L The molar mass of riboflavin is 376.36 g/mol: $$c = 3.975 \times 10^{-5} \times 376.36 \times 1000 = 14.96 \text{ mg/L}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Number of Theoretical Plates (Van Deemter Concept) The efficiency of a chromatographic column is measured by the number of theoretical plates $N$. For a Gaussian peak with retention time $t_R$ and baseline width $W_b$: ### Step 1: Define peak width For a Gaussian peak, the standard deviation $\sigma$ relates to baseline width as: $$W_b = 4\sigma$$ ### Step 2: Number of theoretical plates formula The number of theoretical plates is defined based on the ratio of retention time to peak dispersion: $$N = \left(\frac{t_R}{\sigma}\right)^2$$ ### Step 3: Substitute $\sigma = W_b / 4$ $$N = \left(\frac{t_R}{W_b/4}\right)^2 = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{W_b}\right)^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Higher $N$ means a more efficient column with sharper peaks — critical for resolving complex food matrices. ## 3.4 Worked Example — Resolution Calculation **Problem:** In an HPLC analysis of two preservatives (sorbic acid and benzoic acid), the retention times are $t_{R1} = 4.2$ min and $t_{R2} = 5.8$ min, with baseline widths of $W_1 = 0.4$ min and $W_2 = 0.5$ min. Calculate the resolution. ### Step 1: Apply the resolution formula $$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{W_1 + W_2}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute the values $$R_s = \frac{2(5.8 - 4.2)}{0.4 + 0.5}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the result $$R_s = \frac{2 \times 1.6}{0.9} = \frac{3.2}{0.9} = 3.56$$ ### Step 4: Interpretation Since $R_s > 1.5$, the two preservatives are **baseline separated** — an excellent result for food quality control. ## 3.5 Retention Factor in TLC — Worked Example **Problem:** A food dye spot travels 6.4 cm on a TLC plate, while the solvent front travels 9.0 cm. Calculate the $R_f$ value. ### Step 1: Apply the $R_f$ formula $$R_f = \frac{d_s}{d_f}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute the values $$R_f = \frac{6.4}{9.0} = 0.711$$ ### Step 3: Interpretation An $R_f$ of 0.711 indicates the dye is relatively non-polar (travels with the mobile phase), useful for comparing with a standard reference. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Spectroscopic Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Food Sample] --> B[Sample Preparation
Homogenization, Extraction] B --> C[Clear Extract / Solution] C --> D[Energy Source
UV Lamp, IR Source, Flame] D --> E[Interaction with Sample
Absorption or Emission] E --> F[Detector
Photomultiplier, CCD, Coil] F --> G[Signal Processor
Amplifier, Computer] G --> H[Spectrum / Data Output
Identify and Quantify] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Chromatographic Separation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Food Sample Injection] --> B[Mobile Phase
Liquid or Gas] B --> C[Stationary Phase
Column or Plate] C --> D{Separation Based On} D --> E1[Adsorption] D --> E2[Partition] D --> E3[Ion Exchange] D --> E4[Size Exclusion] E1 --> F[Detector] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G[Chromatogram
Peaks vs Time] G --> H[Qualitative ID
by Retention Time] G --> I[Quantitative Analysis
by Peak Area] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — UV-Vis Spectrophotometer Components ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Light Source
Deuterium or Tungsten Lamp] --> B[Monochromator
Prism or Grating] B --> C[Beam Splitter] C --> D[Reference Cell
Solvent Blank] C --> E[Sample Cell
Food Extract in Cuvette] D --> F[Detector 1
Reference] E --> G[Detector 2
Sample] F --> H[Signal Comparator
Log Amplifier] G --> H H --> I[Readout
Absorbance Value] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — HPLC System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Solvent Reservoir
Mobile Phase] --> B[Degasser] B --> C[Pump
High Pressure] C --> D[Injector
Sample Loop] D --> E[Column
RP-C18 Typical] E --> F[Detector
UV, DAD, FLD, RID, MS] F --> G[Data System
Chromatogram] H[Waste] --> E ``` ## 4.5 Schematic — GC-MS Hyphenated System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Liquid Food Extract] --> B[Injector
Split/Splitless] B --> C[GC Oven
Capillary Column] C --> D[Carrier Gas
He or H2] D --> C C --> E[MS Detector
Electron Impact Ionization] E --> F[Mass Analyzer
Quadrupole] F --> G[Detector
Electron Multiplier] G --> H[Mass Spectrum
m/z vs Intensity] ``` ## 4.6 Comparison Matrix — Major Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Food Analysis Methods] --> B[Spectroscopy] A --> C[Chromatography] B --> B1[UV-Vis: Pigments, Preservatives] B --> B2[FTIR: Oils, Adulteration] B --> B3[AAS: Heavy Metals] B --> B4[NMR: Authentication] B --> B5[MS: Residues] C --> C1[TLC: Colors, Toxins] C --> C2[GC: Volatiles, FAMEs] C --> C3[HPLC: Vitamins, Preservatives] C --> C4[IEC: Organic Acids] C --> C5[CE: Chiral Additives] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Differentiate between AAS and AES with respect to their principle and application in food analysis.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | AAS (Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy) | AES (Atomic Emission Spectroscopy) | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Measures absorption of specific wavelength light by ground-state free atoms | Measures light emitted when excited atoms return to ground state | | **Energy Source** | Hollow cathode lamp (specific to element) | Flame, ICP, or arc | | **State of Atoms** | Ground state | Excited state | | **Multi-element** | Single element at a time | Simultaneous multi-element | | **Sensitivity** | Very high (ppb level) | High (ppm-ppb) | | **Food Application** | Quantification of lead, cadmium, arsenic in food | Multi-element screening of trace minerals in food | AAS is more sensitive for individual elements, while AES/ICP-OES is preferred when multiple elements need to be analyzed simultaneously in a food sample. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Explain the principle of HPLC and list two food components commonly analyzed by HPLC.** **Model Answer:** **Principle of HPLC:** High-Performance Liquid Chromatography separates components of a food mixture based on their differential distribution between a liquid mobile phase (pumped at high pressure, typically 50–400 bar) and a stationary phase (packed in a column with particles of 3–10 μm diameter). Components with weaker affinity for the stationary phase elute first; those with stronger affinity elute later. Detection is done using UV-Vis, fluorescence, or mass spectrometry detectors. **Two food components analyzed by HPLC:** 1. **Water-soluble vitamins** (Vitamin B-complex, Vitamin C) — using RP-HPLC with UV detection. 2. **Synthetic preservatives** (benzoic acid, sorbic acid, BHA, BHT) — using RP-HPLC with UV detection at 230–280 nm. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by: (1) confusing Beer-Lambert Law with Beer-Law only and forgetting the path length factor; (2) writing applications without specifying detection wavelength or detector type; (3) failing to draw labeled diagrams — **always include a labeled block diagram for full marks**; (4) mixing up GC and HPLC working principles; (5) not mentioning specific food examples for each technique. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the principle, instrumentation, and applications of **UV-Visible Spectroscopy** in food analysis. State Beer-Lambert Law and explain each term. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle, working, and food applications of **Gas Chromatography (GC)**. What is a GC-FID and why is it used for fatty acid analysis? --- ### Model Solution — Question A (a) **Principle of UV-Vis Spectroscopy:** UV-Visible spectroscopy is based on the absorption of ultraviolet (190–400 nm) and visible (400–800 nm) radiation by molecules containing chromophores (such as conjugated π-electron systems, aromatic rings, and certain functional groups). When the energy of incident photons matches the energy gap between electronic energy levels of the molecule, absorption occurs, and the wavelength at which maximum absorption occurs (λ_max) is characteristic of that molecule. **Beer-Lambert Law:** $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ Where: - $A$ = Absorbance (dimensionless) - $\varepsilon$ = Molar absorptivity or molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹) - $c$ = Concentration of the absorbing species (mol/L) - $l$ = Path length of the cuvette (cm) **Instrumentation (Block Diagram):** ``` Light Source (D2 for UV, W for Visible) → Monochromator (Grating) → Beam Splitter → Sample Cell (Cuvette) + Reference Cell → Detector (Photomultiplier/Photodiode) → Amplifier → Recorder/Computer ``` **Components and their Functions:** 1. **Light Source:** Deuterium lamp (190–400 nm) for UV; Tungsten-Halogen lamp (350–800 nm) for visible region. 2. **Monochromator:** Isolates a narrow band of wavelengths using a prism or diffraction grating. 3. **Sample Compartment:** Holds the sample in a quartz cuvette (1 cm standard path length). 4. **Detector:** Photomultiplier tube (PMT) or photodiode for measuring transmitted light intensity. 5. **Readout:** Computer or analog meter displays absorbance or transmittance. **Applications in Food Analysis:** - **Pigments:** Chlorophyll (λ_max = 665 nm), β-carotene (450 nm), anthocyanins (520 nm). - **Preservatives:** Benzoic acid (227 nm), sorbic acid (250 nm). - **Total Phenolics:** Folin-Ciocalteu method at 765 nm. - **Protein Estimation:** Lowry or Biuret method at 540–660 nm. - **Color Measurement:** Hunter Lab, CIELAB values for food color quality. - **Adulteration Detection:** Coffee, tea, oils, and milk. --- ### Model Solution — Question A (b) **Principle of Gas Chromatography:** Gas Chromatography separates volatile and semi-volatile components of a food mixture based on their differential partitioning between a gaseous mobile phase (carrier gas — Helium, Nitrogen, or Hydrogen) and a liquid stationary phase (coated on the inner wall of a capillary column or on an inert solid support). Components with higher volatility and lower affinity for the stationary phase elute first. **Block Diagram of GC:** ``` Carrier Gas (He/N2/H2) → Pressure Regulator → Injector (Heated) → GC Oven (Column, Temperature Programmed) → Detector (FID/ECD/MS) → Amplifier → Recorder/Computer (Chromatogram) ``` **Working Procedure:** 1. **Sample Introduction:** Liquid food extract is injected through a septum into a heated injection port, where it vaporizes instantly. 2. **Carrier Gas Flow:** The vaporized sample is swept by the carrier gas into the column. 3. **Separation:** In the column (coiled, 15–30 m long, 0.25–0.53 mm internal diameter), components separate based on their boiling points and interaction with the stationary phase. 4. **Temperature Programming:** The oven temperature is gradually increased to elute compounds of increasing boiling points. 5. **Detection:** Eluting compounds are detected and recorded as peaks in the chromatogram. **GC-FID (Flame Ionization Detector):** - The FID uses a hydrogen-air flame to burn the eluting organic compounds. - Carbon atoms produce ions, and the resulting ion current is measured. - It is highly sensitive to organic (carbon-containing) compounds and gives a response proportional to the mass of carbon. - **Why for fatty acid analysis:** Fatty acids are converted to their more volatile **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAMEs)** by derivatization (e.g., with BF₃-methanol), making them suitable for GC. GC-FID provides excellent quantification of FAMEs, which is the gold standard for determining the fatty acid profile of edible oils, dairy fat, and meat. **Food Applications of GC:** - **Fatty acid profiling (FAMEs)** of oils, ghee, butter, meat. - **Volatile flavor compounds** in coffee, spices, fruits, and wine. - **Pesticide residues** (organochlorine, organophosphorus) using GC-ECD. - **Alcohol and ethanol content** in beverages. - **Mycotoxin screening** (e.g., aflatoxins, ochratoxin) using GC-MS. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle and instrumentation of **FTIR Spectroscopy** with its food applications, especially in adulteration detection. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe **HPLC** with a labeled block diagram. Compare **HPLC and GC** based on mobile phase, sample type, detectors, and food applications. --- ### Model Solution — Question B (a) **Principle of FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy):** FTIR spectroscopy is based on the absorption of infrared radiation (4000–400 cm⁻¹) by molecular vibrations. When IR light of energy equal to the vibrational frequency of a chemical bond strikes a molecule, absorption occurs, leading to stretching (symmetric/asymmetric) or bending (scissoring, rocking, wagging, twisting) vibrations. The IR spectrum serves as a unique molecular fingerprint. **Instrumentation of FTIR:** ``` IR Source (Globar) → Michelson Interferometer (Beam Splitter, Fixed Mirror, Moving Mirror) → Sample Compartment → Detector (DTGS or MCT) → Computer with FT Algorithm ``` **Key Components:** 1. **IR Source:** Globar (silicon carbide) or Nernst filament emits broad-spectrum IR. 2. **Michelson Interferometer:** Splits IR beam into two paths; one mirror is fixed and the other moves, creating an interference pattern (interferogram). 3. **Sample Compartment:** Sample is prepared as KBr pellet, Nujol mull, or ATR (Attenuated Total Reflectance) crystal. 4. **Detector:** DTGS (deuterated triglycine sulfate) or MCT (mercury cadmium telluride) detector. 5. **Fourier Transform:** Computer converts the interferogram (time-domain) into a conventional IR spectrum (frequency-domain) using the Fourier Transform algorithm. **Food Applications:** - **Adulteration Detection:** Mixing of cheaper oils (e.g., palm oil) with expensive oils (olive, coconut); detection of synthetic vanillin in natural vanilla; honey adulteration with sugar syrup. - **Quantification:** Moisture, fat, protein, and lactose in dairy products using mid-IR (FTIR) milk analyzers. - **Authentication:** Confirming geographical origin of honey, wine, and cheese. - **Trans-Fat Detection:** Quantification of trans-fatty acids in edible oils. - **Packaging Analysis:** Migration of packaging components into food. --- ### Model Solution — Question B (b) **Principle of HPLC:** HPLC separates components of a food mixture using a high-pressure liquid mobile phase (50–400 bar) pumped through a column packed with microparticulate stationary phase (3–10 μm). Separation is based on adsorption, partition, ion-exchange, or size exclusion. Components are detected by UV, fluorescence, RI, or MS detectors. **Block Diagram of HPLC:** ``` Solvent Reservoir (Mobile Phase) → Degasser → Pump (Isocratic/Gradient) → Injector (Loop/Rheodyne) → Guard Column → Analytical Column (C18/C8) → Detector (UV/DAD/FLD/RID/MS) → Data Processor (Chromatogram) ``` **Comparison Table: HPLC vs. GC:** | Feature | HPLC | GC | |---|---|---| | **Mobile Phase** | Liquid (water, methanol, acetonitrile) | Gas (He, N₂, H₂) | | **Sample Type** | Non-volatile, thermally labile, polar compounds | Volatile, thermally stable compounds | | **Column** | Packed with microparticles (C18, C8, silica) | Capillary column (5%-phenyl-methylpolysiloxane) | | **Operating Temperature** | Ambient to 60°C | 50°C to 400°C (temperature programmed) | | **Detectors** | UV-Vis, DAD, FLD, RID, ELSD, MS | FID, ECD, TCD, MS | | **Food Applications** | Vitamins, preservatives, antioxidants, mycotoxins, sugars, amino acids | Fatty acids (as FAMEs), volatile flavors, pesticides, alcohols | | **Analysis Time** | 5–60 min | 10–60 min | | **Sample Preparation** | Often minimal; can handle aqueous samples | Requires derivatization for non-volatile compounds | **Key HPLC Food Applications:** 1. **Vitamins:** Fat-soluble (A, D, E) and water-soluble (B-complex, C) using RP-HPLC. 2. **Preservatives:** Benzoic acid, sorbic acid, nitrites, BHA, BHT. 3. **Mycotoxins:** Aflatoxins B₁, B₂, G₁, G₂ — using HPLC with post-column derivatization and fluorescence detection. 4. **Sweeteners:** Aspartame, saccharin, sucralose. 5. **Pigments:** Synthetic dyes (Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow) and natural colorants. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Summary for KTU 2024:** - **Spectroscopy = Light-matter interaction; Chromatography = Separation technique.** - **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ — must be written with all three terms and units. - **AAS for heavy metals; ICP-OES for multi-element analysis.** - **FTIR is the gold standard for oil adulteration and dairy compositional analysis.** - **NIR is used for non-destructive, rapid analysis of moisture, fat, and protein in grains and milk.** - **TLC $R_f$ value** = distance traveled by solute / distance traveled by solvent front. - **HPLC is the workhorse** for vitamins, preservatives, antioxidants, and mycotoxins. - **GC is the gold standard** for fatty acid profiling (as FAMEs) and volatile flavor analysis. - **GC-MS / LC-MS** are hyphenated techniques combining separation with mass identification — used for residues and contaminants. - **Number of theoretical plates $N$** is a measure of column efficiency — higher N means sharper peaks. - **Resolution $R_s \geq 1.5$** = baseline separation; below 1.0 = poor separation. - **Always include a labeled block diagram** in 14-mark answers to score full marks. - **Mention specific food examples** (e.g., aflatoxins, BHA, β-carotene) rather than generic statements. - **Remember the KTU syllabus emphasis on:** Beer-Lambert law derivation, FTIR in oil analysis, HPLC in mycotoxin analysis, GC in fatty acid profiling, AAS in heavy metal detection. - **Capacity factor $k'$** is dimensionless and should ideally be between 1 and 10 for good separations. - **NMR** is the gold standard for authentication of fruit juices, wines, and oils. - **FTIR + Chemometrics** is an emerging area for rapid food authentication — high-yield for KTU advanced questions.

topics.title: Refer FSSAI Standard for the Food Product from FSSAI Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT404 - FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Refer FSSAI Standard for the Food Product from FSSAI Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of FSSAI Standards & Regulations

## 1.1 What is FSSAI? — The Formal Academic Definition

The **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** is the apex statutory body established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSS Act, 2006)**, which came into force on **5th August 2011**. It functions under the administrative control of the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India**. The headquarters of FSSAI is located in **New Delhi**.

The principal mandate of FSSAI is to lay down **science-based standards** for articles of food and to regulate their manufacture, storage, distribution, sale, and import, with the objective of ensuring **safe and wholesome food** for human consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI Standard (Definition):** A "Food Safety and Standards Regulation" is a legally enforceable technical specification that prescribes the quality, composition, safety, contaminant limits, labeling requirements, and methods of sampling/testing for a given food product, notified under the FSS Act, 2006.

The principal regulation relevant to Module 4 is the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**, along with its subsequent amendments. Subsidiary regulations cover contaminants, labeling, packaging, sampling, and testing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Traffic Rulebook for Food"

Imagine Indian roads without traffic signals or speed limits. Chaos, right? Now think of **FSSAI standards as the "traffic rulebook for food."** Just as traffic rules specify the maximum permissible speed (analogy: maximum permissible lead content), mandatory signs (analogy: mandatory labeling), prohibited vehicles (analogy: prohibited additives), and accident investigation procedures (analogy: sampling and testing), FSSAI regulations lay down exactly what is allowed, in what quantity, how it must be declared, and how it must be tested.

When a Food Analyst in a NABL-accredited laboratory picks up a sample of, say, **"Refined Sunflower Oil"**, they do not invent their own limits. They open the **"FSSAI Compendium"** (or the FSSAI website) and check the relevant regulation. This is the literal meaning of the instruction **"Refer FSSAI Standard for the Food Product from FSSAI Regulations."**

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning (as defined under FSSAI) |
|------|----------------------------------|
| **Food** | Any substance, whether processed, semi-processed, or raw, intended for human consumption. |
| **Standard** | A specification of composition, quality, or other parameters prescribed under FSS Regulations. |
| **Adulterant** | Any material that makes a food product inferior or harmful, or that misleads the consumer. |
| **Contaminant** | Any substance not intentionally added to food, but present as a residue or production artifact. |
| **Food Additive** | Any substance (not normally consumed as food itself) intentionally added to food for a technological purpose. |
| **Proprietary Food** | An innovative food formulation not covered under any specific standard, requiring FSSAI approval. |
| **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL)** | The legally binding upper limit of an additive/contaminant in a food product. |
| **Labeling** | Any written, printed, or graphic matter present on the label, accompanying the food. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says "Refer FSSAI Standard," the examiner expects you to (1) identify the correct regulation, (2) state the relevant product/parameter, (3) give the numerical limit, and (4) state the method of testing prescribed by FSSAI.

## 1.4 The Three-Tier Regulatory Hierarchy

FSSAI's regulatory framework operates on three tiers, which a student must know to "refer" correctly:

$$\text{Tier 1: Primary Legislation} \rightarrow \text{Tier 2: FSS Regulations} \rightarrow \text{Tier 3: Standards / Manuals / SOPs}$$

1. **Tier 1 — Primary Legislation:** The FSS Act, 2006 (parent law).
2. **Tier 2 — FSS Regulations, 2011 and amendments** (the rulebook):
   - Food Products Standards & Food Additives Regulations
   - Contaminants, Toxins & Residues Regulations
   - Packaging & Labeling Regulations
   - Sampling & Testing (Laboratory Analysis) Regulations
   - Import Regulations
   - Licensing & Registration Regulations
3. **Tier 3 — Specific Standards & Manuals:** Numerical limits, list of permitted additives, prescribed test methods (often aligned with **IS / ISO / AOAC / BIS / Codex** methods).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Architecture of a Typical FSSAI Product Standard

When you "refer" to a standard, you must know what to look for. A typical FSSAI product standard contains the following sub-headings:

1. **Product Name / Description** — the legal name under which the product must be sold.
2. **Essential Composition** — minimum/maximum levels of key ingredients.
3. **Quality Criteria** — sensory, physical, and chemical parameters.
4. **Food Additives Permitted** — list with INS numbers and maximum levels.
5. **Contaminant Limits** — heavy metals, pesticide residues, mycotoxins.
6. **Microbiological Requirements** — total plate count, coliforms, pathogens.
7. **Hygienic Requirements** — plant sanitation, worker hygiene.
8. **Packaging & Labeling** — mandatory declarations, date marking, FSSAI logo & license no.
9. **Method of Sampling & Testing** — the official analytical procedure.
10. **Defects & Tolerance** — the maximum allowable defective units in a lot.

## 2.2 Why "Refer FSSAI Standard" is a Critical Skill in Food Analysis

In a real testing laboratory, the analyst's report is valid **only if the method of testing and the limits of compliance are taken from an officially recognized source.** FSSAI's official methods (published in the *FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods*) are aligned to:

- **BIS / IS (Bureau of Indian Standards / Indian Standards)**
- **AOAC International (Association of Official Analytical Chemists)**
- **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**
- **Codex Alimentarius** (international food code)
- **FSSAI's own in-house methods** for indigenous products (e.g., ghee, paneer, basmati).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix (Common Standards You Must Memorize)

The following table contains **frequently examined** FSSAI limits. Memorize the values marked in bold.

| Food Product | Parameter | FSSAI Limit | Method of Testing |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Pasteurized Milk** | Fat (cow/buffalo) | **Min 3.5% / 5.0%** | Gerber method (IS 1224) |
| **Pasteurized Milk** | Solid-Not-Fat (SNF) | **Min 8.5%** | ISI / BIS |
| **Pasteurized Milk** | Urea (adulterant marker) | **Max 70 mg/100 g** (refined) | FSSAI Manual |
| **Refined Sunflower Oil** | Free Fatty Acid (as oleic) | **Max 0.25%** | IS 548 (Part 1) |
| **Refined Groundnut Oil** | FFA (as oleic) | **Max 0.25%** | IS 548 |
| **Honey** | Moisture | **Max 20%** | Codex / FSSAI |
| **Honey** | HMF (Hydroxymethylfurfural) | **Max 80 mg/kg** | Winkler / Codex |
| **Honey** | Sucrose | **Max 5%** | BIS |
| **Basmati Rice** | Minimum length | **6.61 mm** | Visual / Vernier |
| **Tea (Black)** | Total Ash | **4–8%** | IS 3633 |
| **Chilli Powder** | Lead | **Max 10 ppm** | AAS |
| **Turmeric Powder** | Lead | **Max 10 ppm** | AAS |
| **Bottled Water** | TDS | **Max 500 ppm** | IS 14543 |
| **Bottled Water** | pH | **6.5 – 8.5** | IS 3025 (pH meter) |
| **Carbonated Water** | Brix | **Min 5 °Brix** | Refractometer |
| **All Foods (general)** | Lead (Pb) | **Max 2.5 ppm** (cereals) | AAS/ICP-MS |
| **All Foods (general)** | Cadmium (Cd) | **Max 1.5 ppm** | AAS/ICP-MS |
| **All Foods (general)** | Aflatoxin (total) | **Max 30 µg/kg** | HPLC |
| **Edible Salt (Iodized)** | Iodine (as KI/KIO₃) | **Min 30 ppm at production** | Titration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU:** Questions on this topic often ask you to (a) list the FSSAI limit for a given parameter and (b) state the corresponding method of analysis. Always answer both parts.

## 2.4 The Four Families of FSSAI Regulations Relevant to Analysis

| Family | Notification Number (approx) | Covers |
|--------|------------------------------|--------|
| **Food Products Standards & Food Additives** | F. No. 2-15015/30/2010 | Composition, additives, identity |
| **Contaminants, Toxins & Residues** | F. No. 1-12/Sci. Panel/Notification | Heavy metals, pesticides, mycotoxins |
| **Packaging & Labeling** | F. No. 4/210/Legal/Noti | Date marking, declarations, Veg/Non-veg mark |
| **Laboratory Analysis (Sampling & Testing)** | F. No. P.15014/1/2011 | Designated labs, sampling plans, FSSAI Manual |

> [!NOTE]
> The **FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods (2016, with later amendments)** is the **operational document** a student must refer to when a question says "Refer FSSAI Standard … from FSSAI Regulations." It contains the actual SOPs and methods.

## 2.5 The Logic of "Referencing" — A Three-Step Decision Tree

When a question says "Refer FSSAI Standard for the Food Product from FSSAI Regulations," apply this logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the product category.** Is it a milk product, an edible oil, a cereal, a beverage, a spice, a confection, or a packaged drinking water? This determines which chapter of the *Food Products Standards Regulations* applies.

**Step 2 — Identify the parameter.** Is the question about composition (fat, protein, moisture), a contaminant (lead, aflatoxin), a microbiological parameter (TPC, coliforms), or labeling? This routes you to the *Contaminants Regulations* or the *Lab Analysis Regulations*.

**Step 3 — Quote the limit AND the method.** A complete KTU answer always pairs the **numerical limit** with the **prescribed method of testing** (e.g., "Lead in chilli powder — **Max 10 ppm by Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometry after acid digestion, as per FSSAI Manual Method 1.6, referencing AOAC 999.10**").

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Quality Control Labs (Nestlé, ITC, Britannia, Fonterra):** Every batch is tested against FSSAI limits before release.
- **Third-Party Testing Labs (TUV SUD, SGS, Intertek):** Issue FSSAI-compliance certificates for export and import.
- **Customs / FSSAI Import Clearance:** Imported food lots are held at ports until lab analysis shows FSSAI compliance.
- **E-commerce & Food Safety Officers (FSOs):** Use FSSAI standards as the legal basis for prosecution under Section 26 of the FSS Act.
- **Food Startups:** Use FSSAI's "Proprietary Food" pathway to launch novel products.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Reference Procedure & Worked Example Analysis

## 3.1 The "Refer FSSAI Standard" Procedure — Exhaustive 10-Step Walkthrough

Below is the **canonical procedure** a Food Analyst follows when given a sample and asked to refer to FSSAI standards. Every step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped transitions.

> [!NOTE]
> This procedure is the literal answer expected when a KTU question says "Explain how to refer to the FSSAI standard for any food product from FSSAI regulations."

**Step 1: Receive the food sample** along with a written request specifying the name of the product, batch number, manufacturing date, and the parameters to be tested.

**Step 2: Confirm the legal identity of the product.** Read the label. Note the declared name, ingredient list, net weight, FSSAI license number, and any category claim (e.g., "organic," "low fat," "sugar-free").

**Step 3: Classify the product** under the correct FSSAI regulation chapter. The major chapters of the *Food Products Standards Regulations, 2011* are:

$$\text{Ch. 1: Dairy} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 2: Fats/Oils} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 3: Fruits/Veg} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 4: Cereals} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 5: Bakery} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 6: Beverages} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 7: Sweets/Confectionery} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 8: Spices/Condiments} \;\;|\;\; \text{Ch. 9: Proprietary Foods}$$

**Step 4: Open the *FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods*** (or the relevant BIS/IS/AOAC standard referenced by FSSAI) and locate the section for that product and parameter.

**Step 5: Identify the relevant test method.** Note the method code (e.g., FSSAI Method 1.6 for metals), the apparatus required, reagents, and the principle (titrimetric, gravimetric, spectrophotometric, chromatographic).

**Step 6: Compare the test result with the FSSAI limit** once the analysis is complete. The decision rule is:

$$\text{If } C_{\text{observed}} \leq C_{\text{MPL}} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Compliant (PASS)}$$

$$\text{If } C_{\text{observed}} > C_{\text{MPL}} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Non-Compliant (FAIL)}$$

where $C$ is the concentration of the parameter.

**Step 7: Apply the Sampling Plan** as per the *FSSAI Manual of Sampling for Food Analysis* (typically based on the square-root or hypergeometric plan for defective units).

**Step 8: Document the result** in the prescribed format: name of product, parameter tested, method used, result, limit, PASS/FAIL, analyst signature, date.

**Step 9: For contaminants**, also verify against the *Contaminants, Toxins and Residues Regulations, 2011*. For example, lead limits vary by food category:

$$\text{Fruits \& vegetables} \rightarrow 0.1 \text{ ppm} \quad | \quad \text{Cereals} \rightarrow 2.5 \text{ ppm} \quad | \quad \text{Fish} \rightarrow 0.3 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 10: For labeling**, verify against the *Packaging and Labeling Regulations, 2011* — presence of FSSAI logo + license no., Veg/Non-veg symbol, date marking (Best Before / Date of Manufacture), nutritional info, allergen declaration, and a declaration of all added colors and flavors.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Referring to FSSAI Standard for "Pasteurized Cow Milk"

**Given:** A sample labelled "Pasteurized Cow Milk, Toned" is submitted for analysis. The analyst must test it for Fat, SNF, Urea, and Aflatoxin M1.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step (a) — Legal classification:**
The product is a "Pasteurized Milk" under **Regulation 4 of FSSAI (Food Products Standards & Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**, Chapter 1 (Dairy Products).

**Step (b) — Fat content (chemical analysis):**
Use the **Gerber method (IS 1224:1977, reaffirmed 2014)**, which is the official test for fat in milk.

$$\text{Fat (\% v/w)} = \frac{\text{Volume of fat column in butyrometer} \times 0.1173}{\text{Weight of sample}}$$

The butyrometer is read directly; a 10.0 g sample with a 0.1 mL read = 1.175 % fat (calibration is built into the butyrometer). For the "Toned" variant (a type of pasteurized milk with a lower fat standard), the FSSAI limit is:

$$\text{Fat}_{\min} = 3.0\% \quad | \quad \text{SNF}_{\min} = 8.5\%$$

**Step (c) — SNF (Solid-Not-Fat) content:**
Calculated using the **Richmond's formula**:

$$\text{SNF (\%)} = \frac{\text{Lactometer Reading (LR)}}{4} + 0.25 \times \text{Fat (\%)} + 0.5$$

Or measured by **gravimetric oven-drying method (IS 12333)**.

**Step (d) — Urea content (adulterant marker):**
FSSAI method based on the **modified DAM (Diacetyl Monoxime) method**, reference FSSAI Manual 2016.

$$\text{Urea (mg/100g)} \leq 70 \quad \text{(Refined product limit)}$$

**Step (e) — Aflatoxin M1 (contaminant):**
Refer to *Contaminants, Toxins and Residues Regulations, 2011*. For milk:

$$\text{Aflatoxin M1}_{\max} = 0.5 \; \mu\text{g/kg} \; (0.5 \; \text{ppb})$$

**Method:** HPLC with fluorescence detection after immunoaffinity column clean-up (FSSAI Manual, equivalent to AOAC 2000.08).

**Step (f) — Compilation of findings:**

| Parameter | Method | FSSAI Limit | Result | Compliance |
|-----------|--------|-------------|--------|------------|
| Fat (Gerber) | IS 1224 | $\geq 3.0\%$ | 3.4% | PASS |
| SNF (Richmond) | Calculation | $\geq 8.5\%$ | 8.6% | PASS |
| Urea | DAM method | $\leq 70$ mg/100g | 42 mg/100g | PASS |
| Aflatoxin M1 | HPLC-FLD | $\leq 0.5$ µg/kg | 0.08 µg/kg | PASS |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Different Foods Are Routed to Different Standards

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sample} &\xrightarrow{\text{label reading}} \text{Product Category} \\
\text{Product Category} &\xrightarrow{\text{FSSAI Regulations lookup}} \text{Applicable Chapter} \\
\text{Chapter} &\xrightarrow{\text{parameter search}} \text{Method of Analysis} \\
\text{Method} &\xrightarrow{\text{lab execution}} \text{Result} \\
\text{Result} &\xrightarrow{\text{comparison with MPL}} \text{Pass / Fail}
\end{aligned}$$

This linear flow is the **mental model** a KTU student must internalize. It directly mirrors the structure of a typical 14-mark ESE question on "Refer FSSAI Standard for [X]."

## 3.4 Special Case — Proprietary Foods

If a product is **not covered by any existing FSSAI standard** (e.g., a novel plant-based meat analogue), the manufacturer must apply for a **"Proprietary Food" approval** under **Regulation 2.1.1 (2.1.1.1)** of the Food Products Standards Regulations. In this case, the reference becomes the **"approval letter"** issued by FSSAI, which prescribes ingredient and additive limits specific to that product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Proprietary Food" with "Novel Food." Novel Food ingredients (e.g., insects, novel proteins) require a separate **"No Objection Certificate (NOC)"** from FSSAI before being used in any food product.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics of the FSSAI Reference System

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The FSSAI Reference Architecture

Below is a Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow showing how a Food Analyst refers to FSSAI standards during analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Received in Lab] --> B[Read Label and Product Name]
    B --> C{Product Identified?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Open FSSAI Manual of Methods]
    C -- No --> E[Classify as Proprietary Food]
    E --> F[Check FSSAI Approval Letter]
    D --> G[Food Products Standards Reg 2011]
    D --> H[Contaminants Reg 2011]
    D --> I[Packaging and Labeling Reg 2011]
    G --> J[Identify Parameter Limit]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K[Identify Prescribed Method]
    K --> L[Perform Laboratory Analysis]
    L --> M[Compare Result with MPL]
    M --> N{Compliant?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Issue PASS Certificate]
    N -- No --> P[Issue FAIL Notice and Reject Lot]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Routing a Sample to the Correct Regulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Incoming Food Sample] --> Q1{Is the product covered by a specific FSSAI standard?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Refer Food Products Standards and Food Additives Reg 2011]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Apply for Proprietary Food / Novel Food Approval]
    Q2 --> R1{Contaminant or residue testing required?}
    R1 -- Yes --> R2[Refer Contaminants Toxins and Residues Reg 2011]
    R1 -- No --> R3{Is it a packaged product?}
    R3 -- Yes --> R4[Refer Packaging and Labeling Reg 2011]
    R3 -- No --> R5[Apply General Hygiene Reg 2011]
    R2 --> T1[Consult FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods 2016]
    R4 --> T1
    R5 --> T1
    T1 --> Z[Issue Analytical Report with PASS or FAIL Decision]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Reference Documents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[FSS Act 2006 Parent Law] --> REG1[Food Products Standards and Food Additives Reg 2011]
    TOP --> REG2[Contaminants Toxins and Residues Reg 2011]
    TOP --> REG3[Packaging and Labeling Reg 2011]
    TOP --> REG4[Sampling and Testing Reg 2011]
    REG1 --> MAN1[FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis 2016]
    REG2 --> MAN1
    REG3 --> MAN1
    REG4 --> MAN1
    MAN1 --> REF1[Referenced BIS and IS Standards]
    MAN1 --> REF2[Referenced AOAC International Methods]
    MAN1 --> REF3[Referenced ISO and Codex Methods]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** ESE questions often test whether you can *trace a parameter from the parent Act → the regulation → the Manual → the test method*. The arrows in the diagram above show that exact path.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define FSSAI. What is the role of FSSAI in setting food standards in India?**

**Model Answer:**
FSSAI stands for the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**, established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, operational since **5th August 2011**, headquartered in **New Delhi** under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.

**Role of FSSAI:**
1. Lays down science-based standards for articles of food.
2. Regulates manufacture, storage, distribution, sale, and import of food.
3. Ensures safe and wholesome food for human consumption.
4. Notifies food testing laboratories and approves test methods.
5. Promotes food safety awareness and training.
6. Coordinates with international bodies (Codex, WTO-SPS).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any three FSSAI Regulations under the FSS Act, 2006, and state their scope.**

**Model Answer:**

| Regulation | Scope |
|------------|-------|
| 1. Food Products Standards and Food Additives Regulations, 2011 | Prescribes identity, composition, quality, and permitted additives for all named food products. |
| 2. Contaminants, Toxins and Residues Regulations, 2011 | Sets maximum permissible limits for heavy metals, pesticide residues, mycotoxins, and other contaminants. |
| 3. Packaging and Labeling Regulations, 2011 | Mandates declarations on the label, date marking, nutritional information, and the FSSAI logo/license number. |

(Other acceptable answers: Sampling and Testing Regulations; Import Regulations; Licensing and Registration Regulations.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice]`

**Q: (a) Explain the procedure to refer to the FSSAI standard for a given food product from the FSSAI Regulations. (7 marks)**
**(b) As a Food Analyst, you receive a sample labelled "Refined Sunflower Oil" for FSSAI compliance testing. Refer to the relevant FSSAI standard, list any five quality parameters, give their FSSAI limits, and state the prescribed method of analysis. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Procedure to refer FSSAI standard:**

The procedure follows a **10-step workflow** (elaborated in Section 3.1 of these notes):

1. Receive the sample and note the product name, batch, and declared parameters.
2. Read the label and confirm the product identity.
3. Classify the product under the correct chapter of the *Food Products Standards and Food Additives Regulations, 2011* (Dairy / Oils / Cereals / Beverages / Spices / etc.).
4. Open the *FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods (2016)* and locate the relevant section.
5. Identify the **test method** (BIS / IS / AOAC / ISO / FSSAI in-house).
6. Perform the analysis in the lab.
7. Compare the observed result with the **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL)** given in the FSSAI regulation.
8. Apply the sampling plan from the *Sampling and Testing Regulations*.
9. Document and sign the report — **PASS** if within limit, **FAIL** if exceeding the limit.
10. For contaminants and labeling, cross-verify against the *Contaminants Regulations* and the *Packaging & Labeling Regulations* respectively.

**Part (b) — Refined Sunflower Oil — FSSAI parameters:**

The product falls under **Regulation 12 (Refined Vegetable Oils)** of the *Food Products Standards and Food Additives Regulations, 2011*, Chapter 2 (Fats and Oils).

| S.No. | Parameter | FSSAI Limit (Refined Sunflower Oil) | Method of Analysis |
|-------|-----------|-------------------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Free Fatty Acid (as oleic acid) | **Max 0.25%** | Titrimetric — IS 548 (Part 1) |
| 2 | Moisture and Volatile Matter | **Max 0.10%** | Hot-air oven, IS 548 |
| 3 | Refractive Index (at 40 °C) | 1.4640 – 1.4690 | Abbe refractometer |
| 4 | Iodine Value | 100 – 145 (g I₂/100 g) | Wijs method — IS 548 |
| 5 | Saponification Value | 188 – 194 (mg KOH/g) | AOAC / IS 548 |
| 6 | Colour (Lovibond, 1 inch cell) | Not specified in FSSAI but must be pale yellow | Lovibond Tintometer |
| 7 | Unsaponifiable Matter | **Max 1.5%** | IS 548 |
| 8 | Presence of Argemone oil (adulterant) | **Absent** | Spot test / HPLC |

(Any five of the above suffice for 7 marks.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice]`

**Q: (a) Describe in detail the structure and key clauses of the FSSAI (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011, with examples. (7 marks)**
**(b) What is the role of the FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods? How is it referenced during routine food analysis? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Structure of FSSAI (FPSFA) Regulations, 2011:**

The regulations are organized into **chapters by food category**, with each chapter containing specific standards for products within that category.

- **Chapter 1 — Dairy Products:** Standards for milk, toned milk, double-toned milk, skimmed milk, pasteurized milk, condensed milk, milk powder, ghee, butter, cheese, ice cream, khoa, paneer.
  - *Example:* Pasteurized cow milk must contain **minimum 3.5% fat and 8.5% SNF.**
- **Chapter 2 — Fats, Oils and Fat Emulsions:** Standards for ghee, refined and unrefined edible oils (groundnut, sunflower, mustard, coconut, palm, olive, soyabean), vanaspati.
  - *Example:* Refined mustard oil must have **FFA (as oleic) max 0.25% and saponification value 168–181.**
- **Chapter 3 — Fruits and Vegetables:** Standards for jams, jellies, marmalades, fruit juices, canned fruits, dehydrated vegetables, pickles.
- **Chapter 4 — Cereals and Cereal Products:** Standards for atta, maida, semolina, corn flakes, biscuits, bread, pasta.
- **Chapter 5 — Bakery Products:** Standards for bread, biscuits, cakes, rusks.
- **Chapter 6 — Beverages:** Standards for tea, coffee, bottled water, packaged drinking water, carbonated water, fruit-based beverages.
- **Chapter 7 — Sweets and Confectionery:** Standards for sugar, jaggery, honey, confectionery, cocoa products.
- **Chapter 8 — Spices, Condiments, and Related Products:** Standards for black pepper, cardamom, cumin, turmeric, chilli, coriander, curry powder, mustard, vinegar.
- **Chapter 9 — Proprietary Foods:** Standards for novel/innovative foods not covered elsewhere.

Each entry contains the **essential composition, quality criteria, permitted additives (with INS numbers and maximum levels), and labelling requirements.**

**Part (b) — Role of the FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods (2016):**

The FSSAI Manual is the **operational document** that specifies **how** a food parameter is to be measured in a designated testing laboratory. It is referenced during analysis as follows:

1. **Method Selection:** The analyst locates the parameter and refers to the prescribed method code (e.g., FSSAI Method 1.6 for heavy metals in spices).
2. **Apparatus and Reagents:** The Manual lists the exact glassware, instruments (AAS, HPLC, GC, FTIR), and reagent grades required.
3. **Procedure:** Stepwise SOP is followed — sample preparation, extraction, digestion, clean-up, measurement, and calculation.
4. **Calculation:** Worked examples are provided, e.g., for Aflatoxin by HPLC:
$$\text{Aflatoxin (µg/kg)} = \frac{A_{\text{sample}} \times C_{\text{std}} \times V \times D}{A_{\text{std}} \times W}$$
   where $A$ = peak area, $C_{\text{std}}$ = standard concentration, $V$ = final volume, $D$ = dilution factor, $W$ = sample weight.
5. **Compliance Check:** The final value is compared with the **MPL** in the relevant regulation.
6. **Quality Control:** The Manual also gives guidance on **recovery, repeatability, and reproducibility** of the method.
7. **Cross-references:** Most FSSAI methods cite an external standard (BIS, IS, AOAC, ISO) so the analyst can consult the parent document if required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not quote a wrong limit.** A common error is quoting the **CODEX** limit instead of the **FSSAI** limit. Always use the *Food Safety and Standards Regulations* value, not the international default.
> 2. **Do not omit the method.** Stating "Aflatoxin max 30 µg/kg" without mentioning "HPLC with immunoaffinity clean-up" will cost you at least 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Do not confuse sub-product limits.** The FSSAI limit for lead in *edible oils* is **0.5 ppm**, but in *spices* it is **10 ppm**. Generic quoting of "lead max 10 ppm" for all foods is a **fatal** mistake.
> 4. **Always specify the regulation name in full at first mention**, e.g., "FSSAI (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011" — not just "FSSAI limit."
> 5. **For honey:** Do not quote the European limit (HMF max 40 mg/kg) — FSSAI's HMF limit is **80 mg/kg**. Similarly, sucrose is **5%** under FSSAI, not 10%.
> 6. **For milk adulteration:** Urea is **max 70 mg/100 g** only for *refined* products. For *raw* milk, refer to the FSSAI Manual for the specific cut-off.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FSSAI = Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**, statutory body under FSS Act 2006, HQ New Delhi, since 2011.
- **"Refer FSSAI Standard"** means → identify the product → open the *FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis of Foods, 2016* → apply the **limit AND the method**.
- The principal regulation is the **Food Products Standards and Food Additives Regulations, 2011**, with subsidiary regulations for **Contaminants**, **Packaging & Labeling**, and **Sampling & Testing**.
- The standard structure is: **Product Description → Essential Composition → Quality Criteria → Additives → Contaminant Limits → Microbiology → Hygiene → Packaging/Labeling → Sampling → Test Method**.
- **High-yield FSSAI limits to memorize:**
  - Pasteurized milk: Fat **≥ 3.5% (cow) / 5.0% (buffalo)**, SNF **≥ 8.5%**, Urea **≤ 70 mg/100g**.
  - Refined edible oil: FFA (as oleic) **≤ 0.25%**, Moisture **≤ 0.1%**.
  - Honey: Moisture **≤ 20%**, HMF **≤ 80 mg/kg**, Sucrose **≤ 5%**.
  - Aflatoxin (general foods): **≤ 30 µg/kg**; Aflatoxin M1 (milk): **≤ 0.5 µg/kg**.
  - Lead in cereals **≤ 2.5 ppm**, Lead in spices **≤ 10 ppm**, Lead in edible oils **≤ 0.5 ppm**.
  - Bottled water: TDS **≤ 500 ppm**, pH **6.5–8.5**.
- **Methods are always quoted alongside limits.** Common methods: Gerber (fat), Richmond (SNF), Wijs (iodine value), AOAC 999.10 (lead), HPLC-FLD (aflatoxin), BIS 14543 (bottled water).
- **Proprietary Foods** (novel items not in any standard) need a separate **FSSAI approval letter** — they are not covered by default.
- **KTU 14-mark answer strategy:** (i) Introduce the regulation with full name; (ii) Quote the limit; (iii) Quote the method; (iv) Give a worked example with PASS/FAIL decision.
- **Always cross-check labelling:** FSSAI logo + license no. + Veg/Non-veg mark + date marking + nutritional info are mandatory under *Packaging & Labeling Regulations, 2011*.
- **Sampling rule of thumb:** $n = \sqrt{N}$ (square-root plan) for most lot sizes, where $n$ is the number of incremental samples to draw from a lot of $N$ units.
- **Stay current:** FSSAI frequently amends limits (e.g., Aflatoxin in spices was reduced from 30 to 20 µg/kg in some sub-categories). Always check the **latest gazette notification** before answering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compare the Result of Analysis with FSSAI Standards and Infer the Result
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT404 - FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compare the Result of Analysis with FSSAI Standards and Infer the Result

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Comparison of analytical results with FSSAI standards** is the systematic, legally-defensible procedure of evaluating the numerical data obtained from physico-chemical, microbiological, and sensory analyses of a food sample against the **prescribed limits, maximum permissible limits (MPLs), tolerance limits, and quality criteria** laid down in the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**, the **Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Regulations, 2011**, and the **Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020** — as amended from time to time by the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSS Act, 2006)**.

**Inference** is the final, evidence-based regulatory conclusion drawn from the comparison, which classifies the sample as either:
- **Conforming (Compliant / Pass)** — when the analytical value lies **within** the permitted FSSAI range.
- **Non-Conforming (Non-Compliant / Fail)** — when the analytical value **exceeds the maximum permissible limit** or falls **below the minimum prescribed limit** for the specified parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every analytical result reported in a B.Tech Food Technology project / viva / ESE question must be interpreted in the **legal context of FSSAI**. A test result by itself has **no statutory meaning** until it is benchmarked against an FSSAI standard.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence Speed Limit" Model

Imagine you drive a car with a **speedometer reading of 80 km/h** on a road. The number 80 is meaningless until you ask: *"What is the speed limit here?"*

- If the board says **"Maximum 60 km/h"** → you are **NON-COMPLIANT** → challan (penalty).
- If the board says **"Minimum 60 km/h – Maximum 100 km/h"** → you are **COMPLIANT** → legal drive.

Here:
- **Speedometer reading** = your **analytical result** (e.g., moisture = 14.2 %).
- **Traffic sign / speed limit** = **FSSAI Standard** (e.g., FSSAI says moisture in wheat atta ≤ 14.0 %).
- **Challan / Legal drive** = **Inference (Fail / Pass).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Golden Questions for Every Result:**
> 1. **WHAT** did I measure? (parameter)
> 2. **WHAT** does the law say? (FSSAI limit)
> 3. **SO WHAT** does my value mean legally? (inference)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| FSSAI statutory body | **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**, MoHFW, GOI |
| Parent legislation | **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (No. 34 of 2006)** |
| Statutory limit notation | **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL)** in mg/kg or ppm or % |
| Test decision threshold | **Reported value vs. MPL**, with measurement uncertainty |
| Confidence level (legal) | **95 %** (k = 2) for regulatory enforcement |
| Sample conformity basis | **FSSAI Manual of Methods of Analysis** (latest edition) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of FSSAI Standards

FSSAI regulates food through **four principal regulatory frameworks**, and your analytical result must be matched against the correct one:

1. **Food Products Standards (FPS)** — identity, quality, and composition (e.g., fat % in milk, moisture in butter, ash in spices).
2. **Food Additives** — permitted colours, preservatives, antioxidants and their **INS numbers** with **Maximum Permitted Levels (MPLs)**.
3. **Contaminants, Toxins & Residues** — heavy metals (Pb, As, Cd, Hg), mycotoxins (Aflatoxin), pesticides, melamine.
4. **Microbiological Standards** — TPC, coliform, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, yeast & mould limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **form of the standard** dictates the **form of the comparison**: for **contaminants** it is a **one-sided upper limit** ("value ≤ MPL"); for **additives** it is also **upper**; but for **composition (e.g., milk fat)** it is often a **two-sided window** ("min ≤ value ≤ max").

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Comparison

1. **Identify the food matrix** and the **specific regulation** applicable (e.g., milk → FSSAI sub-regulation 2.1.1; edible oil → 2.2).
2. **Locate the parameter** in the FSSAI table for that food (e.g., "Baudouin test – negative" for groundnut oil).
3. **Note the unit and limit type** (mg/kg, % w/w, ppm, cfu/g, Absence in 25 g).
4. **Apply uncertainty correction** (optional, advanced) to decide if the value is "statistically distinguishable" from the MPL.
5. **Declare inference** as **COMPLIANT** or **NON-COMPLIANT**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Decision Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical pipe `|` symbol is intentionally replaced with `\vert` so that the markdown table does not break.

| # | Comparison Type | Decision Rule | Inference Symbol | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Upper limit (contaminant) | If $C_{\text{analytical}} \le \text{MPL}$ | ✅ Compliant | Pb in milk $\le$ 0.02 mg/kg |
| 2 | Upper limit (additive) | If $C_{\text{analytical}} \le \text{MPL}$ | ✅ Compliant | Benzoic acid in sauce $\le$ 750 mg/kg |
| 3 | Lower limit (composition) | If $C_{\text{analytical}} \ge \text{Min}$ | ✅ Compliant | Milk fat $\ge$ 3.5 % |
| 4 | Two-sided window | If $\text{Min} \le C_{\text{analytical}} \le \text{Max}$ | ✅ Compliant | TSS in jam (45–75 %) |
| 5 | Range (qualitative) | If $C_{\text{analytical}} \in [\text{Min}, \text{Max}]$ | ✅ Compliant | Moisture in atta 13–14 % |
| 6 | Absence test | If detected = "Not Detected" (ND) | ✅ Compliant | *Salmonella* in 25 g |
| 7 | Exceeds MPL | $C_{\text{analytical}} > \text{MPL}$ | ❌ Non-Compliant | Aflatoxin > 30 µg/kg |
| 8 | Below minimum | $C_{\text{analytical}} < \text{Min}$ | ❌ Non-Compliant | Curd fat < 3.0 % |

### 2.3.1 The Measurement-Uncertainty-Corrected Decision Rule

In legally disputed cases, the regulatory rule becomes:

$$
C_{\text{reported}} \pm U_{95} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{MPL}
$$

where $U_{95}$ is the **expanded measurement uncertainty at 95 % confidence**.

- **Compliant (with certainty)** if: $\quad C_{\text{reported}} + U_{95} \le \text{MPL}$
- **Non-Compliant (with certainty)** if: $\quad C_{\text{reported}} - U_{95} > \text{MPL}$
- **Inconclusive / "Case of doubt"** if: $\quad C_{\text{reported}} - U_{95} \le \text{MPL} < C_{\text{reported}} + U_{95}$ — the **"Shared Risk Zone"** (rarely asked in B.Tech, but high-yield for viva).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This comparison protocol is the **backbone of**:
- **FSSAI Food Safety Officer (FSO) enforcement** in markets and ports.
- **NABL-accredited testing labs** issuing **Legal Compliance Certificates**.
- **Export certification** to EU/US/Codex markets.
- **Industry in-house Quality Assurance (QA)** during production.
- **Food safety recalls** (e.g., the 2023–2024 spice recall due to ethylene oxide).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstrations

## 3.1 Worked Demonstration 1 — Aflatoxin in Groundnut (Contaminant, One-Sided Upper Limit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Model answer style for 14-mark questions).**

A groundnut sample was analysed by **HPLC-FLD** (immunoaffinity column clean-up) for total Aflatoxin (B1+B2+G1+G2). The lab reported:

$$
C_{\text{reported}} = 34.2 \; \mu\text{g/kg}, \qquad U_{95} = 4.1 \; \mu\text{g/kg}
$$

The FSSAI limit under the **Contaminants Regulations, 2011 (Reg. 2.3.1)** for "groundnut, oilseeds and nuts intended for further processing" is:

$$
\text{MPL}_{\text{Aflatoxin}} = 30 \; \mu\text{g/kg}
$$

### Step 1 — Write the comparison

$$
C_{\text{reported}} \pm U_{95} = 34.2 \pm 4.1 \; \mu\text{g/kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the lower and upper bounds

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{lower}} &= C_{\text{reported}} - U_{95} \\
&= 34.2 - 4.1 \\
&= 30.1 \; \mu\text{g/kg} \\
\\
C_{\text{upper}} &= C_{\text{reported}} + U_{95} \\
&= 34.2 + 4.1 \\
&= 38.3 \; \mu\text{g/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the decision rule (without uncertainty)

$$
34.2 > 30 \implies \text{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the uncertainty-corrected decision rule

Even using the **most favourable (lowest) bound**:

$$
C_{\text{lower}} = 30.1 \; \mu\text{g/kg} > \text{MPL} = 30 \; \mu\text{g/kg}
$$

Therefore, the sample is **NON-COMPLIANT with statistical certainty**.

### Step 5 — Final Inference (in KTU answer-script style)

> The analytical value of 34.2 µg/kg **exceeds the FSSAI maximum permissible limit of 30 µg/kg** for total Aflatoxin in groundnut. **The sample is NON-COMPLIANT (FAIL)** and is **unsafe for human consumption** as per the Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Regulations, 2011. The lot must be **rejected, recalled, or reworked** as per FSSAI enforcement procedure.

## 3.2 Worked Demonstration 2 — Milk Fat % (Two-Sided Window)

A sample of **Toned Milk** was analysed by the **Gerber method** and the fat content was found to be:

$$
\text{Fat}_{\text{analytical}} = 2.85 \;\% \; \text{w/w}
$$

FSSAI standard (FPS Reg. 2.1.1:2) for **Toned Milk** is:

$$
3.0 \;\% \le \text{Fat} \le 4.0 \;\% \quad \text{(minimum 3.0 % m/m)}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify the comparison type

This is a **lower-bound composition** check (the upper bound for fat is rarely violated; minimum is the critical limit).

### Step 2 — Apply the rule

$$
\text{Fat}_{\text{analytical}} = 2.85 \;\% \;<\; \text{Min}_{\text{FSSAI}} = 3.0 \;\%
$$

### Step 3 — Inference

> The sample is **NON-COMPLIANT** because the fat content (2.85 %) is **below the FSSAI minimum of 3.0 %** for toned milk. The product is **misbranded** under the FSSAI labelling regulations, as the declared "toned milk" must contain at least 3.0 % milk fat.

## 3.3 Worked Demonstration 3 — Preservative in Tomato Ketchup

A bottled tomato ketchup was tested for **Sodium Benzoate** by HPLC-UV:

$$
C_{\text{benzoate}} = 820 \; \text{mg/kg}
$$

FSSAI limit (Food Additives Reg. 3.1.4, Table 4) for benzoates in sauces/ketchup is:

$$
\text{MPL} = 750 \; \text{mg/kg}
$$

### Decision

$$
820 \; \text{mg/kg} \;>\; 750 \; \text{mg/kg} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

### Inference

> The benzoate level exceeds the FSSAI MPL by **9.3 %**. The batch is **unsafe**; the manufacturer may be prosecuted under **Section 26 of the FSS Act, 2006** for selling food with prohibited / excess additives.

## 3.4 Worked Demonstration 4 — Microbiological (Absence Test)

A 25 g sample of infant milk substitute was tested for ***Salmonella*** as per **IS 5887 (Part 3)**:

- **Pre-enrichment** in buffered peptone water → **Selective enrichment (Rappaport-Vassiliadis / Tetrathionate)** → **Plating on XLD / BGA** → **Biochemical & serological confirmation**.

**Result observed:** No typical *Salmonella* colonies on XLD after 24 h and 48 h incubation, confirmed negative.

FSSAI microbiological standard (Infant Foods Reg.): ***Salmonella* — Absent in 25 g**.

### Inference

> **COMPLIANT** — *Salmonella* not detected in 25 g; the sample meets the FSSAI microbiological safety criterion for infant foods.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Master Table)

| Parameter Type | Limit Form | Decision Formula (LaTeX-safe) | Typical FSSAI Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Contaminant (upper) | $C \le \text{MPL}$ | $C_{\text{an}} \le \text{MPL}$ | Contaminants Reg., 2011 |
| Additive (upper) | $C \le \text{MPL}$ | $C_{\text{an}} \le \text{MPL}$ | Food Additives Reg., 2011 |
| Composition (lower) | $C \ge \text{Min}$ | $C_{\text{an}} \ge \text{Min}$ | FPS Reg., 2011 |
| Composition (window) | $\text{Min} \le C \le \text{Max}$ | $\text{Min} \le C_{\text{an}} \le \text{Max}$ | FPS Reg., 2011 |
| Microbe (count) | $\text{Count} \le \text{MPL}$ (cfu/g) | $\text{Count} \le \text{MPL}$ | Microbiological Reg. |
| Microbe (absence) | Not Detected in $n$ g | $\text{ND}$ in $n$ g | Microbiological Reg. |
| Pesticide residue | $C \le \text{MRL}$ | $C_{\text{an}} \le \text{MRL}$ | Contaminants Reg., Sch. II |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The "Compare-and-Infer" Pipeline

The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete flow that a B.Tech student must internalise — from **raw sample** to **regulatory inference**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Sample] --> B[Sample Preparation]
    B --> C[Analytical Method Selection]
    C --> D[Instrumental / Wet Chemical Analysis]
    D --> E[Raw Analytical Data e.g. peak area, titre value, count]
    E --> F[Calculation via Calibration / Formula]
    F --> G[Final Analytical Value with Units]
    G --> H{Identify Applicable FSSAI Regulation}
    H --> I[Locate Parameter and MPL in FSSAI Table]
    I --> J{Apply Decision Rule}
    J -->|Value less than or equal to MPL for upper limit| K[COMPLIANT Pass]
    J -->|Value greater than MPL| L[NON-COMPLIANT Fail]
    J -->|Value within Min to Max window| K
    J -->|Value below Min| L
    J -->|Not Detected in n g| K
    J -->|Uncertainty Overlap with MPL| M[Inconclusive Re-sample and Re-test]
    L --> N[Legal Action under FSS Act 2006 Sec 26]
    K --> O[Issue Legal Compliance Certificate]
    M --> P[Send to Referral NABL Lab]
```

## 4.2 Decision-Tree Mermaid — Visualising the Comparison Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Have Analytical Value C_an] --> S1{Is limit an upper bound?}
    S1 -->|Yes, Contaminant or Additive| S2{C_an less than or equal to MPL?}
    S1 -->|No, Composition| S3{Is limit a two-sided window?}
    S3 -->|Yes, Jam, Butter, Atta etc| S4{Min less than or equal to C_an less than or equal to Max?}
    S3 -->|No, only Minimum| S5{C_an greater than or equal to Min?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S6[COMPLIANT]
    S2 -->|No| S7[NON-COMPLIANT]
    S4 -->|Yes| S6
    S4 -->|No| S7
    S5 -->|Yes| S6
    S5 -->|No| S7
    S6 --> S8[Inference: Safe to Release]
    S7 --> S9[Inference: Reject or Recall Lot]
```

## 4.3 Process Schematic — From FSSAI Table Lookup to Inference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FSSAI Regulation Book] --> B[Food Matrix Identification]
    B --> C[Parameter Table Lookup]
    C --> D[Limit Extraction with Unit]
    D --> E[Reported Value in Same Unit]
    E --> F[Apply Comparison]
    F --> G[Inference Pass or Fail]
    G --> H[Documentation and Reporting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When drawing block diagrams in answer sheets, always use **rectangles for steps**, **diamonds for decisions**, and **arrows with single direction**. This is exactly the KTU board examiner's expectation for a "process flow" question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define "Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL)" in the context of FSSAI. Why is it essential to compare every analytical result with the FSSAI MPL before declaring a food sample safe?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 m):** The Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) is the **highest legally acceptable concentration** of a contaminant, additive, pesticide residue or microorganism in a given food matrix, as prescribed in the relevant FSSAI regulations under the FSS Act, 2006.
- **Why it is essential (1.5 m):** A numerical analytical result **by itself has no legal meaning**. Comparing with the FSSAI MPL converts a scientific number into a **statutory inference** (Compliant / Non-Compliant), enabling enforcement, recall or release of the lot, and protecting consumer health.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *List the four main categories of FSSAI regulations against which food analysis results are compared.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Food Safety and Standards (**Food Products Standards and Food Additives**) Regulations, 2011.
2. Food Safety and Standards (**Contaminants, Toxins and Residues**) Regulations, 2011.
3. Food Safety and Standards (**Labelling and Display**) Regulations, 2020.
4. Food Safety and Standards (**Microbiological**) Requirements as notified.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** A sample of **refined groundnut oil** was tested for the **Baudouin test** and was found to be **positive**. The **saponification value** was found to be **185 mg KOH/g** and the **unsaponifiable matter** was **1.2 % w/w**. Compare each result with the relevant FSSAI standards (FPS Regulation 2.2) and infer the overall acceptability of the sample.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **measurement-uncertainty-corrected decision rule** for comparing an analytical result with an FSSAI upper limit. Why is this rule preferred in legal disputes?

### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Comparison with FSSAI Standards for Groundnut Oil (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Analytical Value | FSSAI Standard (FPS Reg. 2.2) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Baudouin test | Positive (red colour) | **Negative** (absence of sesame oil) | **NON-COMPLIANT** |
| Saponification value | 185 mg KOH/g | **188 – 196 mg KOH/g** | **NON-COMPLIANT** (below 188) |
| Unsaponifiable matter | 1.2 % w/w | **Not more than 1.5 % w/w** | **COMPLIANT** |

**Step-by-step reasoning (2 marks for comparison + 2 marks for inference + 1 mark each for two params):**
- **Baudouin test:** A positive result indicates **adulteration of groundnut oil with sesame oil** (≥ 5 %). This is a **legal violation** under FSSAI — Section 26 of FSS Act, 2006.
- **Saponification value:** 185 < 188 → indicates the oil is either **adulterated with a lower-MW oil** (e.g., linseed) or **degraded**. Non-compliant.
- **Unsaponifiable matter:** 1.2 % < 1.5 % → Compliant.

**Overall Inference (1 mark):** The sample is **NON-COMPLIANT** on **two critical parameters** (Baudouin and Saponification value). The lot is **unsafe and misbranded** and must be **rejected by the Food Safety Officer**.

**(b) Measurement-Uncertainty-Corrected Decision Rule (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 m):** Reported analytical value $C_{\text{rep}}$ is never a single point; it carries a measurement uncertainty $U$ at a defined confidence level (usually 95 %, $k = 2$). The true value lies in the interval:

$$
C_{\text{rep}} - U \;\le\; C_{\text{true}} \;\le\; C_{\text{rep}} + U
$$

**Step 2 — Three-way decision (3 m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{If } C_{\text{rep}} + U \le \text{MPL} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COMPLIANT (with certainty)} \\
&\text{If } C_{\text{rep}} - U > \text{MPL} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NON-COMPLIANT (with certainty)} \\
&\text{If } C_{\text{rep}} - U \le \text{MPL} < C_{\text{rep}} + U \;\Rightarrow\; \text{INCONCLUSIVE (shared risk)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Why preferred in legal disputes (2 m):** Without uncertainty, a value of 29.9 µg/kg Aflatoxin (limit 30) would falsely be declared "Compliant" even though measurement scatter could put the true value above 30. The uncertainty-corrected rule is **statistically defensible** in a **FSSAI adjudication** or **court of law**, satisfying the requirements of **ISO/IEC 17025** and **CAC/GL 71-2009**.

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B – Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With a suitable flowchart, describe the **step-by-step procedure** to compare a quantitative food analysis result with the relevant FSSAI standard and infer the result. Use the example of **Lead (Pb) in a sample of infant formula** (analytical value 0.018 mg/kg, expanded uncertainty 0.006 mg/kg, FSSAI MPL 0.02 mg/kg).
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **legal consequences** under the FSS Act, 2006 when a food sample is declared **non-compliant** on comparison with FSSAI standards. Mention the role of the **Food Safety Officer (FSO)** and the **Adjudication process**.

### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Step-by-step Procedure with Flowchart and Worked Example (7 marks)**

**Flowchart (drawn in 3 marks):**

```
Sample → Method Selection → Analysis → Reported Value (C_rep, U)
       → Identify FSSAI Regulation → Extract MPL
       → Compare using C_rep ± U
       → Inference (Pass / Fail / Inconclusive)
       → Documentation
```

**Worked Example (4 marks):**

Given: $C_{\text{rep}} = 0.018$ mg/kg, $U = 0.006$ mg/kg, $\text{MPL} = 0.02$ mg/kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{lower}} &= 0.018 - 0.006 = 0.012 \; \text{mg/kg} \\
C_{\text{upper}} &= 0.018 + 0.006 = 0.024 \; \text{mg/kg} \\
\text{MPL} &= 0.020 \; \text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $C_{\text{lower}} = 0.012 < 0.020 < C_{\text{upper}} = 0.024$ → **MPL lies INSIDE the uncertainty interval**.
- **Decision:** **INCONCLUSIVE / Shared Risk Zone**.
- **Inference:** Cannot be declared compliant with certainty. The lot must be **re-sampled and re-tested by an NABL-accredited referral lab** (as per FSSAI Manual of Methods).

**(b) Legal Consequences and Role of FSO (7 marks)**

- **Section 26, FSS Act, 2006 (2 m):** Manufacture, storage, sale or distribution of food that is **unsafe, misbranded, sub-standard or containing excess contaminants/additives** is a **punishable offence** with imprisonment up to **6 months and fine up to ₹5 lakh** (first offence), and up to **lifetime imprisonment and ₹10 lakh** for repeated / dangerous violations.
- **Role of FSO (2 m):** The Designated Officer (DO) and FSO are empowered to **draw samples, send to NABL lab, issue improvement notice, seize the article**, and file prosecution before the **Adjudicating Officer** or **Food Safety Appellate Tribunal**.
- **Adjudication (2 m):** The **Adjudicating Officer** hears both parties, evaluates the analytical report (with uncertainty), and may impose **penalty up to ₹10 lakh** (for non-unsafe but sub-standard food) and order **destruction or recall**.
- **Right of appeal (1 m):** Aggrieved parties may appeal to the **Food Safety Appellate Tribunal** and then to the **High Court**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Writing only the analytical value, NOT the FSSAI limit** (loses 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting the units** (mg/kg ≠ ppm — 1 mg/kg = 1 ppm in dilute aqueous systems, but **NOT in fats**; always quote FSSAI units).
> 3. **Not stating the regulation reference** (e.g., "Contaminants Reg. 2011, Schedule II").
> 4. **Ignoring measurement uncertainty** in close-to-MPL values (this is a 2-mark "differentiator" question in ESE).
> 5. **Concluding "the food is unsafe" without citing the FSSAI section** that defines the violation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Comparison = Benchmarking:** Analytical value must always be matched against the **specific FSSAI regulation and sub-regulation** for the food matrix.
- **Four FSSAI regulatory pillars:** Food Products Standards, Additives, Contaminants, Microbiological.
- **Limit types:** Upper (contaminants, additives), Lower (composition minimum), Two-sided (composition window), Absence (microbes in $n$ g).
- **Decision rule (basic):** $C_{\text{an}} \le \text{MPL}$ → Compliant; $C_{\text{an}} > \text{MPL}$ → Non-Compliant.
- **Decision rule (advanced, uncertainty-corrected):**
  - $C_{\text{rep}} + U \le \text{MPL}$ → Compliant (certain)
  - $C_{\text{rep}} - U > \text{MPL}$ → Non-Compliant (certain)
  - $C_{\text{rep}} - U \le \text{MPL} < C_{\text{rep}} + U$ → **Inconclusive** (re-sample, re-test).
- **Inference vocabulary:** Compliant / Pass / Safe → release; Non-Compliant / Fail / Unsafe → reject, recall, prosecute under **Section 26, FSS Act 2006**.
- **Always quote** the **specific regulation number, parameter, and unit** in your answer.
- **Baudouin positive = sesame oil adulteration** in groundnut oil — classic KTU trick question.
